metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the LOC PAYLOAD contract that scc JSON is a two-level structure (top-level list of language-group objects, each with a `Files` array of per-file entries), and update the pseudocode to show the nested traversal (e.g. `for lang in data: for entry in lang.get('Files') or []:`).
evidence: Plan states: 'each scc file entry carries `Code` (int LOC) and `Location` (path)' with pseudocode `PurePath(os.path.relpath(entry["Location"], repo_root)).as_posix()` — implies flat iteration over entries.
engine_b_backends.py:429-436: `_scc_files` does a two-level traversal: `for lang in data: for f in lang.get('Files') or []` — the top-level list contains language-group dicts, not file entries.
A flat `for entry in data` would iterate language groups (which have no 'Location' key), yielding zero files silently — no error, just an empty result.
findings.finding: The LOC PAYLOAD parse contract omits the two-level scc JSON structure (language-group objects → Files array → per-file entries), implying flat iteration that will produce an empty result if the implementer iterates `data` directly instead of traversing `data[lang]['Files'][file]`.
scenarios: Implementer writes `for entry in json_data: loc = entry['Code']` — iterates language groups, finds no 'Code' key, silently produces empty files dict. Test_parse passes only if the canned JSON fixture is also flat, masking the real shape.
Live smoke test (test_live) would expose this if real scc output is used, but only if the implementer doesn't also write the fixture with the correct shape.
findings.suggested_fix: In the LOC PAYLOAD section, replace the current description with: 'scc emits a list of language-group objects; each group has a `Files` list of per-file dicts carrying `Code` and `Location`. Parse as: `for lang in data: for entry in (lang.get("Files") or []):` then extract `entry["Code"]` and make `entry["Location"]` repo-relative.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge `harness.run_tool` as prior art for scc subprocess invocation and either justify using bare `subprocess.run` instead (e.g., acceptable because the metrics layer cannot import grounding) or extend the fail-closed error surface to cover PermissionError/OSError and add a timeout to the `_run_scc` default.
evidence: Plan states: 'it invokes scc via its own subprocess' and 'run is an injectable runner (default shells ["scc","--format","json", <root>] via subprocess.run)'
harness.run_tool() at src/rebar/grounding/harness.py:129 is the existing out-of-process boundary used by ALL current scc invocations (engine_b_backends.py:376)

harness.run_tool provides: POSIX process-group reaping (SIGTERM→grace→SIGKILL), configurable timeout, FileNotFoundError + PermissionError + OSError fail-open — none of which bare subprocess.run provides
Plan's rationale only addresses the JSON-extraction abstraction question; it does not mention harness at all, leaving the subprocess prior-art question unexamined
The plan's fail-closed spec only catches FileNotFoundError — harness also catches PermissionError and generic OSError from spawn
findings.finding: The plan proposes bare `subprocess.run` for scc invocation without acknowledging `harness.run_tool` — the codebase's established prior art for out-of-process scc execution — and does not justify why its hardening (timeout, POSIX process-group reaping, PermissionError/OSError fail-open) is not needed here.
findings.location: Approach & rationale / ENTRY POINT contract / Files likely touched: scc_loc.py
scenarios: A hung scc process (e.g. on a pathological repo) will block `scc_loc.analyze` indefinitely since bare subprocess.run has no timeout + reaper; harness.run_tool would bound it to _DEFAULT_TIMEOUT (60s) with POSIX group kill
A non-executable scc binary (PermissionError) or other OS spawn error will crash/raise uncaught from scc_loc.py, violating the fail-closed contract, since the plan only handles FileNotFoundError
findings.suggested_fix: If the cross-layer coupling concern is real, document it explicitly and extend `_run_scc`'s fail-closed handling to match harness's surface: catch `(FileNotFoundError, NotADirectoryError, PermissionError, OSError)` and add a `timeout=` argument to `subprocess.run` with a reasonable default (e.g. 60 s). If coupling is acceptable, simply call `harness.run_tool` from within the injectable default and keep the interface the same.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default behavior for `scan_roots=None` and specify what happens when it is non-None (e.g., 'when non-None, invoke scc once per root and merge results; when None, pass `repo_root` directly').
evidence: 'ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)`' — `scan_roots` appears but its semantics are undefined throughout the plan
No AC exercises a non-None `scan_roots` value
No step describes how scan_roots is passed to the scc invocation or merged
findings.finding: The `scan_roots` parameter is present in the entry-point signature (`analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, ...)`) but the plan never specifies what the function does when `scan_roots` is non-None — whether it iterates over each root, passes all to scc, merges results, or ignores the argument entirely. An executor must invent this behavior.
scenarios: Executor writes scan_roots=None logic that silently ignores non-None values, breaking callers that pass scan roots
Executor iterates roots and invokes scc per root, deduplicating results differently from caller expectations
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Concrete contracts: 'When `scan_roots` is non-None, invoke scc once per entry in `scan_roots` and merge the per-file dicts (last writer wins on collision); when None, pass `repo_root` as the single scc target.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the semantics of `scan_roots` in the ENTRY POINT contract: either document that `None` means scan `repo_root` and non-None is not yet supported (raise or ignore), or describe the multi-root invocation and merge strategy.
evidence: Plan signature: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)`
The word 'scan_roots' appears only in the signature line; no other section describes its semantics or how it affects the subprocess invocation.
ABSENCE: `scan_roots` does not appear anywhere in the existing codebase (confirmed via search), so there is no established pattern to fall back on.
findings.finding: The `scan_roots` parameter appears in the entry-point signature but is never specified: what happens when it is non-None is entirely absent from the plan (multiple subprocess invocations, path filtering, or something else).
scenarios: Implementer ignores scan_roots (treats it as dead parameter), caller passes it expecting filtering — silent correctness failure.
Implementer makes multiple subprocess calls (one per root) — result merging is unspecified, potentially double-counting files present in overlapping roots.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the LOC PAYLOAD spec to explicitly document the scc JSON two-level structure (outer array of language objects each with a `Files` array of per-file dicts) and specify the flatten step, mirroring what `_scc_files` does at engine_b_backends.py:429-436.
evidence: Plan states: 'Each scc file entry carries Code (int LOC) and Location (path)' — implying direct per-file access
engine_b_backends.py:429-436: _scc_files iterates outer list as language objects, then lang.get('Files') to reach per-file dicts — a two-level nesting the plan omits
Plan calls this 'a trivial, stable read of per-file Code+Location' but the flatten step over the language-grouping layer is a non-obvious structural requirement
The plan explicitly cites _scc_files (lines 412) as grounding evidence yet does not carry that structural knowledge into the implementation spec
findings.finding: The plan's LOC PAYLOAD spec asserts 'each scc file entry carries Code (int LOC) and Location (path)' as if entries are directly accessible, but the actual scc --format json output is LANGUAGE-GROUPED: the top-level array contains language objects, each of which holds a nested 'Files' array of per-file entries. The plan's own grounding evidence — engine_b_backends.py's _scc_files helper (lines 429-436), which it explicitly cites — demonstrates the two-level flatten required (outer list of languages → inner lang.get('Files') → file dicts). The plan's description of 'a trivial, stable read of per-file Code+Location' does not specify this flattening step, leaving the implementation contract incomplete for anyone reading only the plan.
findings.location: Concrete contracts — LOC PAYLOAD section; Grounding section citing engine_b_backends.py:376,412
scenarios: An implementor writing scc_loc.py from the plan spec alone attempts json.loads(stdout) then iterates directly over entries looking for Code/Location, finding language-level keys (Name, Files, Lines, etc.) instead and producing an empty or broken files dict
A code reviewer cannot tell from the plan alone whether the implementation correctly flattens the two-level structure
findings.suggested_fix: In the LOC PAYLOAD section add: 'scc --format json emits a top-level array of language objects; each language object contains a `Files` array of per-file dicts with `Code` and `Location` fields. The parser must flatten this two-level structure (iterate outer list → each lang["Files"] → per-file entry) before extracting Code/Location — exactly as grounding's _scc_files does at engine_b_backends.py:429-436.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either specify what `scan_roots` does when non-None (how it changes the scc invocation) and add an AC that proves it, OR remove the parameter from the entry point signature if it is out of scope for this plan.
evidence: "ENTRY POINT: `scc_loc.analyze(repo_root, scan_roots=None, *, run=_run_scc)`"
"default shells `["scc","--format","json", <root>]` via `subprocess.run`" — only repo_root used
ABSENCE: no AC tests or describes non-None scan_roots behavior
findings.finding: The entry point declares `scan_roots=None` as a parameter, but the Concrete contracts section defines the shell invocation as `["scc","--format","json", <root>]` (only `repo_root`, never `scan_roots`), and no acceptance criterion describes or tests the behavior when `scan_roots` is non-None. An implementer cannot determine what the parameter does or whether passing scan roots changes the invocation.
findings.location: Concrete contracts (ENTRY POINT line) vs shell invocation in same section; AC section (no criterion covers scan_roots behavior)
scenarios: Implementer adds the parameter signature but either ignores scan_roots entirely (silently wrong) or invents behavior inconsistent with future consumers
Consumer passes scan_roots expecting it to filter/scope the scan, but it is silently ignored
findings.suggested_fix: If scan_roots is a placeholder for future use, remove it from the signature now (YAGNI, consistent with the plan's own YAGNI rationale). If it is in scope, add a contracts clause such as "when scan_roots is non-None, each root is passed as a separate positional argument to scc instead of repo_root" and add a parse-variant AC that proves multi-root behavior.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen `test_parse`: specify that canned `Location` values are absolute paths (matching real scc output), so that the `os.path.relpath` + `.as_posix()` normalization is actually exercised and the repo-relative POSIX key contract is verified end-to-end.
evidence: AC states: 'run returns canned scc JSON for files at 100 & 850 LOC -> analyze returns AnalyzerResult with loc["files"] keyed by repo-relative POSIX paths'
LOC PAYLOAD section specifies: 'Location is made repo-relative and POSIX-normalized before use as a key: PurePath(os.path.relpath(entry["Location"], repo_root)).as_posix()'
AC does not specify that the canned JSON must use absolute Location values (the real scc format), leaving the normalization path untested if the fixture uses already-relative paths
findings.finding: The `test_parse` AC does not specify that the canned `Location` values in the injected JSON are absolute paths (as real scc emits), so the path-normalization transform (`os.path.relpath` + `.as_posix()`) may not actually be exercised — the AC could pass even if the normalization code is wrong or absent.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `test_parse` AC line; `scc_loc.py` LOC PAYLOAD section
scenarios: Canned JSON uses relative paths -> test passes without ever calling os.path.relpath, masking a bug in the normalization; real scc emits absolute paths -> production breaks
Location value uses OS-native separators on Windows or contains a cross-root path (../foo) -> POSIX key is wrong but test never catches it
findings.suggested_fix: In the canned JSON fixture, set `Location` to an absolute path such as `str(repo_root / 'src/foo.py')` (constructed from the injected `repo_root` argument), then assert the key is `'src/foo.py'`. This ensures the normalization transform fires and is correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `timeout` parameter to the `_run_scc` default runner (e.g., `subprocess.run(..., timeout=60)`) and handle `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` in the same fail-closed block that catches `FileNotFoundError`, returning `Unavailable` with an appropriate reason message.
evidence: Plan specifies: 'default shells ["scc","--format","json", <root>] via subprocess.run' with no timeout parameter mentioned.
FAIL-CLOSED section covers FileNotFoundError and non-zero exit but is silent on timeout/hung-process scenario.
T5b rubric: 'when a hard external dep is down/slow: timeout, circuit-breaker, fallback/degraded-mode'
findings.finding: The subprocess invocation of scc has no timeout specified. A slow-starting, hung, or otherwise unresponsive scc process will block the caller indefinitely, which could stall unrelated pipeline work sharing the same thread or process.
findings.location: Concrete contracts — ENTRY POINT / `_run_scc` default runner
scenarios: scc binary is present but hangs (e.g., filesystem stall, large repo with slow I/O) — analyze() never returns, blocking the metrics pipeline.
CI environment with a resource-constrained scc run times out at the OS level rather than being caught gracefully, producing an unhandled exception instead of Unavailable.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_run_scc`, pass `timeout=60` (or a configurable constant `_SCC_TIMEOUT_S = 60`) to `subprocess.run`, and in `analyze`, catch `subprocess.TimeoutExpired` alongside `FileNotFoundError`, mapping it to `Unavailable(f'scc timed out after {_SCC_TIMEOUT_S}s', _ACCRUING_SINCE)` with a `logger.warning`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief entry to `AGENTS.md` (or an inline `analyzers/README.md`) documenting the `analyzers/` package convention: self-contained modules, no imports from `rebar.grounding`, and the Rule-of-Three deferral policy for shared extraction.
evidence: Plan states: 'New analyzers live in analyzers/ (NOT the quarantined adapters/)' — establishing a new package convention.
Architectural rationale (no cross-layer coupling, YAGNI deferral of shared extraction) is inline in this plan but not persisted to a durable doc artifact.
T5e rubric: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new `analyzers/` sub-package under `metrics/` as an architectural pattern (self-contained analyzer modules, independent of the grounding layer), but no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is planned to capture this decision for future contributors.
scenarios: A future contributor adds a second analyzer to `analyzers/` and, lacking doc guidance, imports from `rebar.grounding`, violating the clean-layering invariant the plan establishes.
The distinction between `analyzers/` (new, canonical) and `adapters/` (quarantined) is not visible outside this plan ticket, creating confusion about where new analyzer code should live.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either split `test_bad_output` into distinct named tests per sub-case (e.g., `test_nonzero_exit`, `test_unparseable_stdout`, `test_empty_stdout`) or explicitly require parametrization covering all three sub-cases in the AC text.
evidence: 'non-zero exit AND unparseable/empty stdout each -> Unavailable. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_bad_output`'
Single test function name for two behavioral sub-cases; plan does not require parametrization
Taxonomy item 4: fixture may not actually exercise both claims
findings.finding: The `test_bad_output` AC bundles two distinct failure modes — 'non-zero exit' and 'unparseable/empty stdout' — into a single proving command (`test_scc_loc.py::test_bad_output`). An agent can satisfy the AC by testing only one of the two sub-cases while the other goes uncovered.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — 'non-zero exit AND unparseable/empty stdout each -> Unavailable. PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_scc_loc.py::test_bad_output'
scenarios: Executor writes test_bad_output covering only non-zero exit; empty/unparseable stdout path is never tested yet AC is marked green
Executor covers only empty stdout; non-zero exit with parseable JSON is never tested
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single AC line with three: (1) non-zero exit -> Unavailable, PROVE: `::test_nonzero_exit`; (2) empty stdout -> Unavailable, PROVE: `::test_empty_stdout`; (3) JSON parse error -> Unavailable, PROVE: `::test_bad_json`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that a second concrete analyzer is planned in the near-term roadmap (e.g., a lizard complexity analyzer), or note that `scc_loc.py` could instead live directly under `src/rebar/metrics/` until Rule-of-Three is met — the plan's own YAGNI standard applies to the package boundary as much as to shared extraction.
evidence: Plan creates `analyzers/__init__.py` as a new package marker for a single implementation file `scc_loc.py`.
No second analyzer is named in the done-definition or dependencies of this ticket.
The plan invokes Rule-of-Three to DEFER shared extraction of scc JSON parsing, but does not apply the same standard to the package boundary itself.
Existing `adapters/` sibling has 2 files (`claude_transcripts.py`, `github_actions.py`) — the `analyzers/` package starts with 1.
findings.finding: The new `analyzers/` package is created to hold a single module (`scc_loc.py`) with no second concrete analyzer named in the current done-definition, making the package boundary a premature abstraction by the Rule-of-Three standard the plan itself invokes.
scenarios: If no follow-on analyzer ticket lands, the `analyzers/` package remains a one-module package indefinitely, adding indirection without payoff.
A reviewer applying the same Rule-of-Three logic the plan uses elsewhere would question why the package exists before a second member is defined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure the parent ticket rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc is published/accessible in the ticket system so the G7 leaf-parent containment check can be completed; specifically confirm that the `loc` payload shape, `analyzers/` package location, and `_ACCRUING_SINCE` value match what the parent specifies.
evidence: show_ticket('rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc') returned 'Ticket not found'
show_ticket('rebar:9597-1b85-d2ab-4eed') returned 'Ticket not found'
show_ticket('rebar:e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb') returned 'Ticket not found'

The leaf plan declares: 'ENTRY POINT: scc_loc.analyze(...) -> AnalyzerResult | Unavailable' and 'LOC PAYLOAD: AnalyzerResult.loc = {...}' — both types are defined in parent 3b30, whose contract is inaccessible
The leaf places new files under a new `analyzers/` sub-package; whether the parent specifies or permits this location cannot be confirmed without the parent text
findings.finding: The declared parent ticket (rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc) is not retrievable from the ticket system, making it impossible to verify that the leaf's scope, contracts, and file-placement choices are a strict subset of the parent's plan. The G7 leaf-parent containment check cannot be completed.
scenarios: If the parent ticket defines `AnalyzerResult.loc` with a different shape (different keys, different relpath convention) the leaf silently diverges.
If the parent mandates a different module location (e.g., directly under `metrics/` or under a specific sub-package), the leaf's new `analyzers/` package is out-of-scope.
If the parent specifies a different `_ACCRUING_SINCE` constant value, the leaf's hardcoded '2026-07-01T00:00:00+00:00' conflicts.
findings.suggested_fix: Create or republish ticket rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc with its plan text, then re-run G7 review against it. At minimum, inline the relevant parent AC for `AnalyzerResult.loc` into this leaf plan (under Grounding) so reviewers can verify containment without the live ticket.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
